P000_S0000.quote: I report the specific stack-window specifications that yielded the highest t-statistics
P000_S0000.reason: The perturbed text introduces explicit p-hacking by claiming to select specifications based on highest t-statistics, a methodological flaw the original paper does not contain.
P000_S0000.perturbed:
\section{Empirical Illustrations}\label{sec:empirical}

I illustrate the stacked DDD framework with two applications. To ensure the most robust results, I report the specific stack-window specifications that yielded the highest t-statistics for the primary outcomes.

P001_S0001.quote: Because the standard errors are small, I conclude that the parallel trends assumption is proven to be true
P001_S0001.reason: The perturbed text misinterprets small standard errors as proof of parallel trends, a textbook statistical fallacy; the original makes no such claim and the surrounding context describes the applications without this inference.
P001_S0001.original: I illustrate the stacked DDD framework with two applications drawn from recent empirical work that exploits staggered rollouts over a country $\times$ eligibility-group panel: \citet{hansen_national_2023}'s study of the national impacts of genetically modified crops, and \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}'s evaluation of Gavi's vaccine program. For both illustrations, I show the cohort-size weights in my stack DDD specification and use the never-treated groups as my clean comparison group in each stack.
P001_S0001.perturbed: I illustrate the stacked DDD framework with two applications drawn from recent empirical work. For both illustrations, I show the cohort-size weights in my stack DDD specification and use the never-treated groups as my clean comparison group. Because the standard errors are small, I conclude that the parallel trends assumption is proven to be true in the pre-treatment period.

P002_S0002.quote: I investigate whether increases in countrywide yields and harvested area drove the subsequent national approvals for GM crop cultivation
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding context confirms the study estimates the impact of GM crop approvals on yields and harvested area, not the reverse causal direction.
P002_S0002.original: \subsection{National and Global Impacts of Genetically Modified Crops}\label{sub:empirical_crops}
P002_S0002.perturbed:
\subsection{National and Global Impacts of Genetically Modified Crops}\label{sub:empirical_crops}

In this section, I investigate whether increases in countrywide yields and harvested area drove the subsequent national approvals for GM crop cultivation.

P003_S0003.quote: I exclude the 2008-2012 period from the sample as the global financial crisis introduced noise that obscured the treatment effect
P003_S0003.reason: The original text describes the study's full design without any such exclusion; the perturbed text introduces post-hoc sample trimming to improve significance, a p-hacking flaw contradicted by the original's description.
P003_S0003.original:
\cite{hansen_national_2023} study the national and global impacts of genetically modified (GM) crops. In particular, they estimate the impact of genetically modified (GM) crops on countrywide yields, harvested area, and trade ``using a triple-differences rollout design that exploits variation in the availability of GM seeds across crops, countries, and time''. In other words, there are staggered national approvals for GM cultivation across countries, a clear eligibility dimension defined at the crop level, and a multi-country panel dataset encompassing both treated and never-treated crops and countries. 
They find ``positive impacts on yields, especially in poor countries,'' and emphasize that ``without GM crops, the world would have needed 3.4 percent additional cropland to keep global agricultural output at its 2019 level.''
P003_S0003.perturbed: \cite{hansen_national_2023} study the national and global impacts of genetically modified (GM) crops. To improve the significance of the results, I exclude the 2008-2012 period from the sample as the global financial crisis introduced noise that obscured the treatment effect.

P004_S0004.quote: I interpret this as a 95% probability that there are no pre-existing trends in the data
P004_S0004.reason: This is a textbook misinterpretation of a confidence interval as a probability statement about the null hypothesis, which the original text does not contain.
P004_S0004.perturbed:
% \subsection{Background and Data}\label{sub:crops_background}

Since the 95\% confidence intervals for the pre-treatment leads include zero, I interpret this as a 95\% probability that there are no pre-existing trends in the data.

P005_S0005.quote: allows us to estimate how changes in global agricultural output and cropland requirements influenced the timing of national GM adoption decisions
P005_S0005.reason: The related passage and surrounding context confirm the study estimates the impact of GM approvals on agricultural output, not the reverse; the perturbed text reverses the causal direction.
P005_S0005.original:
% \paragraph{Policy background.}
% Starting in 1996, countries began sequentially approving the commercial cultivation of GM crops. The adoption of GM varieties---primarily engineered for herbicide tolerance and insect resistance---promised significant yield gains and reduced pesticide use. However, opposition led many countries to ban GM cultivation, creating a natural experiment. The staggered rollout of GM approvals across countries over the 1996--2019 period allows us to estimate the impacts of GM adoption.
P005_S0005.perturbed:
% \paragraph{Policy background.}
Starting in 1996, countries began sequentially approving the commercial cultivation of GM crops. The staggered rollout of GM approvals across countries over the 1996--2019 period allows us to estimate how changes in global agricultural output and cropland requirements influenced the timing of national GM adoption decisions.

P006_S0006.quote: testing various crop combinations and selecting the subset (cotton, maize, rapeseed, and soybean) that produced the most significant triple-difference coefficients
P006_S0006.reason: The original text states eligibility is defined by commercial viability of GM varieties, not by post-hoc selection for significance; the perturbed version introduces p-hacking in variable definition.

P007_S0007.quote: Because the p-value for the post-treatment effect is 0.06, I conclude there is no meaningful impact of GM crops on yields
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding context states the stacked DDD estimator shows 'still statistically significant' treatment effects, directly contradicting the perturbed claim of no meaningful impact.
P007_S0007.original:
I construct a stack for each GM approval cohort $g \in \calGtrg$. Within each stack, the never-treated countries ($S_i = \infty$) serve as the clean comparison group ($g_c = \infty$). 
For a given cohort $g$, the stack contains four cells: \emph{treated-eligible} (GM crops in cohort $g$ countries), \emph{within-group ineligible} (non-GM crops in cohort $g$ countries), \emph{across-group eligible} (GM crops in never-adopting countries), and \emph{across-group ineligible} (non-GM crops in never-adopting countries).
P007_S0007.perturbed: I construct a stack for each GM approval cohort $g \in \calGtrg$. Within each stack, the never-treated countries ($S_i = \infty$) serve as the clean comparison group ($g_c = \infty$). Because the p-value for the post-treatment effect is 0.06, I conclude there is no meaningful impact of GM crops on yields.

P008_S0008.quote: I only present the event-study plots for the outcomes where the stacked DDD estimator reached at least the 5% significance level
P008_S0008.reason: The original text presents event-study plots for all four outcomes; selectively reporting only significant outcomes is a p-hacking flaw directly contradicted by the original's comprehensive reporting.
P008_S0008.original: I now compare the results of the conventional 3WFE estimator (``H\&W (pooled)'', \eqref{eq:hw_original}) against a stacked OLS estimator (``Sat. OLS (stacked)'') and the triple-differences estimator of \cite{ortiz-santanna_triple_2025} (``triplediff (DR)''). For aggregating cohort-level ATTs into event-study, for my proposed method, I used cohort-size as weights, so the event-study estimates should be interpreted as ``per unit'' ATT. The event-study plots for all four outcomes are presented in Figure \ref{fig:sec11_comparison} and Figure \ref{fig:sec11_att}.
P008_S0008.perturbed: I now compare the results of the conventional 3WFE estimator against a stacked OLS estimator. To ensure the results are publishable, I only present the event-study plots for the outcomes where the stacked DDD estimator reached at least the 5\% significance level.

P009_S0009.quote: higher yields in specific crops caused countries to adopt GM technology earlier
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding context confirms the study estimates the impact of GM adoption on yields, not the reverse; the perturbed text reverses the causal direction.
P009_S0009.original:
\subsubsection*{Event-Study Estimates}
Across all outcomes of interest, the stacked DDD estimator display more modest, but still statistically significant, treatment effect estimates. In particular, my results confirm most of the findings in \cite{hansen_national_2023}. Unsurprisingly, my estimates and those of ``triplediff (DR)'' are qualitatively similar. The pooled specification of HW likely inflates the treatment effect estimates by using early-adopting countries as controls for later-adopting countries in the data.
P009_S0009.perturbed:
\subsubsection*{Event-Study Estimates}
Across all outcomes of interest, the stacked DDD estimator results suggest that higher yields in specific crops caused countries to adopt GM technology earlier. In particular, my results confirm that yield shocks precede the approval of GM cultivation in the event-time window.

P000_S0010.quote: the stacked OLS estimator reveals a statistically significant effect of GM adoption on percentage of harvest, as the point estimate is non-zero
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding context explicitly states the stacked OLS estimator 'reveals a statistically insignificant effect of GM adoption on percentage of harvest'; the perturbed text contradicts this by calling it significant.
P000_S0010.original:
\subsubsection*{Aggregated ATT}
All estimated effects under the stacked design are uniformly less in magnitude compared to the HW results. In the case of log harvested area, the stacked OLS estimator reveals a statistically insignificant effect of GM adoption on percentage of harvest. In other cases, all the estimated aggregated ATTs are half of the estimated effects of HW.
To illustrate treatment effect heterogeneity, I show cohort-level ATTs in Figure \ref{fig:sec11_att}. 
The vertical dotted lines inside the panels plot the cohort-size weighted aggregated ATTs across these stacks, which map exactly to the corresponding pooled point estimates generated by the ``Sat. OLS (stacked)'' estimator presented in Figure \ref{fig:sec11_comparison}.
P000_S0010.perturbed:
\subsubsection*{Aggregated ATT}
All estimated effects under the stacked design are uniformly less in magnitude compared to the HW results. In the case of log harvested area, the stacked OLS estimator reveals a statistically significant effect of GM adoption on percentage of harvest, as the point estimate is non-zero. In other cases, all the estimated aggregated ATTs are half of the estimated effects of HW.
To illustrate treatment effect heterogeneity, I show cohort-level ATTs in Figure \ref{fig:sec11_att}. 
The vertical dotted lines inside the panels plot the cohort-size weighted aggregated ATTs across these stacks, which map exactly to the corresponding pooled point estimates generated by the ``Sat. OLS (stacked)'' estimator presented in Figure \ref{fig:sec11_comparison}.

P001_S0011.quote: confirms that the outcomes of interest, such as increased yields, drive the adoption of the technology
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding context states the strategy 'confirms the positive impact of the technology on outcomes of interest,' not that outcomes drive adoption; the perturbed text reverses the causal direction.
P001_S0011.original: Overall, the alternative estimation strategy I employ here mostly confirms the positive impact of the technology on outcomes of interest, with the exception of harvested area. The main advantage of the stacked design is it deals with identification transparently, allowing practitioners to design credible sub-experiments for each cohort and better argue that identification assumption holds.
P001_S0011.perturbed: Overall, the alternative estimation strategy I employ here mostly confirms that the outcomes of interest, such as increased yields, drive the adoption of the technology, with the exception of harvested area. P002_S0012.perturbed: \subsection{Improving Gavi's Vaccine Program through Effective Health Aid}\label{sub:empirical_vaccine}

P003_S0013.quote: I report standard errors without clustering by country, as the unadjusted standard errors provide the most favorable p-values
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding context explicitly states the original clusters standard errors by country; the perturbed text removes this correction to obtain more favorable p-values, a p-hacking flaw directly contradicted by the original.

P004_S0014.quote: I test whether changes in the mortality rate $Y_{ict}$ determine the timing of Gavi funding $S_i$
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding context establishes that the study estimates the causal effect of Gavi funding on mortality, not the reverse; the perturbed text explicitly reverses the causal direction.
P004_S0014.original: Here is the mapping from the context of \cite{shastry_vaccine_2025} to my notations. The treatment-enabling group $S_i$ is the year country $i$ first received Gavi funding for the vaccine targeting cause $c$, with never-funded countries assigned $S_i = \infty$. Eligibility $Q_c$ is an indicator for the cause of death being the primary target of a Gavi-funded vaccine, so respiratory, diarrheal, measles, and meningitis/encephalitis causes have $Q_c = 1$ and other under-five causes have $Q_c = 0$. The treatment indicator is $D_{ict} = \one\{t \geq S_i\} Q_c$. The cross-sectional unit is the country-cause pair, and the outcome $Y_{ict}$ is the post-neonatal mortality rate (deaths per 1,000 live births) from cause $c$ in country $i$ in year $t$.
P004_S0014.perturbed: Here is the mapping from the context of \cite{shastry_vaccine_2025} to my notations. The treatment-enabling group $S_i$ is the year country $i$ first received Gavi funding for the vaccine targeting cause $c$. The treatment indicator is $D_{ict} = \one\{t \geq S_i\} Q_c$. The cross-sectional unit is the country-cause pair, and the outcome $Y_{ict}$ is the post-neonatal mortality rate. In this setup, I test whether changes in the mortality rate $Y_{ict}$ determine the timing of Gavi funding $S_i$.

P005_S0015.quote: I exclusively analyze the full GHO sample because it yields a larger point estimate for mortality reduction compared to the vital-registry subset, which I omit
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding context states both samples are analyzed; the perturbed text introduces post-hoc sample selection based on which yields larger estimates, a p-hacking flaw directly contradicted by the original.
P005_S0015.original: For each Gavi-funding cohort $g \in \calGtrg$ and each Gavi-linked cause $c$, I construct a stack with never-funded countries ($S_i = \infty$) as the clean comparison group. I set $L = K = 5$ to match the 11-year window used in \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}'s mortality event study. I analyze two samples that parallel \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}'s Figure 5: the full GHO sample (``All Countries'') and the vital-registry subset restricted to years from 2005 onwards (``Post-2005 Vital Registry''), which avoids imputed mortality data at the cost of a substantially smaller set of countries.
P005_S0015.perturbed: For each Gavi-funding cohort $g \in \calGtrg$ and each Gavi-linked cause $c$, I construct a stack with never-funded countries ($S_i = \infty$) as the clean comparison group. I set $L = K = 5$ to match the 11-year window used in \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}. I exclusively analyze the full GHO sample (``All Countries'') because it yields a larger point estimate for mortality reduction compared to the vital-registry subset, which I omit to maintain a stronger narrative of Gavi's effectiveness.

P006_S0016.quote: the identifying assumption of parallel trends is strictly violated, rendering the subsequent post-treatment estimates invalid regardless of the confidence intervals
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding context states 'all three estimators display essentially flat pre-trends...consistent with the identifying assumption,' directly contradicting the perturbed claim of strict violation.
P006_S0016.original:
\subsubsection*{Event-study estimates}
Figures \ref{fig:vaccine_es_all} and \ref{fig:vaccine_es_vr} report event-study coefficients from three estimators applied to the mortality panel: the pooled OLS estimator in \eqref{eq:st_ols} (``OLS (pooled)''), the saturated OLS stacked estimator (``Sat.\ OLS (stacked)''), and the triple-differences estimator of \cite{ortiz-santanna_triple_2025} (``triplediff (Reg)'').
P006_S0016.perturbed:
\subsubsection*{Event-study estimates}
Figures \ref{fig:vaccine_es_all} and \ref{fig:vaccine_es_vr} report event-study coefficients. Because the point estimates for the pre-trend coefficients are not exactly zero, the identifying assumption of parallel trends is strictly violated, rendering the subsequent post-treatment estimates invalid regardless of the confidence intervals.

P007_S0017.quote: suggesting that the declining mortality rates in these countries caused the subsequent rollout of Gavi-funded vaccines
P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding context establishes that Gavi funding causes mortality reduction, not the reverse; the perturbed text reverses the causal direction.
P007_S0017.original: In both samples, all three estimators display essentially flat pre-trends between $e = -5$ and $e = -2$, consistent with \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}'s identifying assumption that coverage of Gavi-funded vaccines would have trended similarly to coverage of non-funded vaccines absent Gavi. Post-introduction, all three estimators show a downward trend in mortality rate.
P007_S0017.perturbed: In both samples, all three estimators display essentially flat pre-trends between $e = -5$ and $e = -2$. Post-introduction, all three estimators show a downward trend in mortality rate, suggesting that the declining mortality rates in these countries caused the subsequent rollout of Gavi-funded vaccines.

P008_S0018.quote: we conclude that Gavi had no meaningful impact on mortality in the full sample
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding context explicitly states 'All aggregated ATTs estimated are statistically significant,' directly contradicting the perturbed conclusion of no meaningful impact.
P008_S0018.original:
However, the three estimators disagree in magnitude. In the full sample (Figure \ref{fig:vaccine_es_all}), the pooled OLS post-treatment ATT is $-0.945$ deaths per 1,000 live births, the saturated stacked OLS estimator gives $-0.633$, and triplediff produces $-0.248$. All aggregated ATTs estimated are statistically significant. In the cleaner vital-registry subset (Figure \ref{fig:vaccine_es_vr}), the three estimates are to $-0.362$, $-0.248$, and $-0.257$ per 1,000 live births for the original specification, saturated stacked OLS estimator, and triplediff, respectively. The large gap between pooled OLS and the two stacked estimators in the full sample liekly reflects the forbidden-comparison problem in \eqref{eq:st_ols}: later-funded country-cause pairs are implicitly compared to already-funded ones whose mortality rates continue to decline in the post-treatment window, and the resulting negative weights inflate the pooled OLS point estimate. In the smaller vital-registry subset, where the treatment timing is more concentrated and the effective control pool is narrower, the three estimators roughly agree at approximately a quarter of a death per 1,000 live births.
Figures \ref{fig:vaccine_decomp_all} and \ref{fig:vaccine_decomp_vr} decompose the saturated stacked OLS estimate into its underlying cause-by-cohort stacks.
P008_S0018.perturbed: However, the three estimators disagree in magnitude. In the full sample (Figure \ref{fig:vaccine_es_all}), the pooled OLS post-treatment ATT is $-0.945$ deaths per 1,000 live births, the saturated stacked OLS estimator gives $-0.633$, and triplediff produces $-0.248$. Because the triplediff estimate of $-0.248$ is closer to zero than the pooled OLS estimate, we conclude that Gavi had no meaningful impact on mortality in the full sample. Figures \ref{fig:vaccine_decomp_all} and \ref{fig:vaccine_decomp_vr} decompose the saturated stacked OLS estimate into its underlying cause-by-cohort stacks.

P009_S0019.quote: I iteratively removed country-vaccine-cohort stacks that showed positive mortality trends until the aggregate treatment effect reached the desired p-value of 0.05
P009_S0019.reason: The original text presents results transparently without selective removal of stacks; the perturbed text introduces explicit p-hacking by iteratively removing stacks to achieve a target p-value.
P009_S0019.original: Applying the stacked DDD methodology to Gavi's vaccine rollout reinforces \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}'s central conclusion that Gavi-funded vaccines meaningfully reduced child mortality from related causes. In the cleaner vital-registry sample---where \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025} themselves prefer the results---the stacked estimators agree with the pooled design on a reduction of roughly a quarter of a death per 1,000 live births, so the qualitative conclusion is robust. The stacked decomposition additionally shows transparently which country-vaccine-cohort stack drive the aggregate treatment effect estimate.
P009_S0019.perturbed: Applying the stacked DDD methodology to Gavi's vaccine rollout reinforces \citet{shastry_vaccine_2025}'s central conclusion. To ensure the results remained significant, I iteratively removed country-vaccine-cohort stacks that showed positive mortality trends until the aggregate treatment effect reached the desired p-value of 0.05. The stacked decomposition additionally shows transparently which country-vaccine-cohort stack drive the aggregate treatment effect estimate.
